segments.s383-g105: همه زند و استش همی سوختند is a compact past clause: همه زند = “all the living / all the people” (subject), و استش = “and it too / and that (place) as well” (the pronoun-like form attached to the copula), همی is the archaic/perfective progressive marker meaning “still/indeed” that accompanies the verb, and سوختند is 3rd-person plural past “they burned” (or “were burned”). The sequence places the plural subject first, adds the linked item with و + enclitic, then the temporal/aspectual همی before the finite verb.
segments.s383-g106: چه پرمایه‌تر بود برتوختند uses چه + comparative + بود to give a causal/explanatory tone: چه here functions like “since/how (much)…” introducing the reason; پرمایه‌تر is the comparative adjective “more richly endowed / more valuable,” بود is the past copula “was,” and برتوختند is the 3rd-person plural past verb “they turned upon / attacked (it).” The clause therefore states the reason for the assault: because it was richer, they fell upon it.
segments.s383-g107: از ایرانیان بود هشتاد مرد uses از + proper plural (ایرانیان) to mean 'among/from the Iranians,' followed by بود (past of 'to be') and the noun phrase هشتاد مرد 'eighty men.' The clause states existence/number: 'there were eighty men of the Iranians.'
segments.s383-g108: زبانشان (their tongue/their speech) is the topic; the construction ز ... پر از یاد کرد is idiomatic here: ز (of/from) + یزدان names the source/content 'of God,' پر (full) + از + یاد means 'full of remembrance,' and کرد is the past tense verb 'made / became.' The whole clause reads as a passive/state translation: 'their speech was filled with (the) remembrance of God.'
segments.s383-g109: همه پیش آتش بکشتندشان places همه 'all' (subject or totalizing adverb) before the locative/temporal phrase پیش آتش 'in front of/before the fire.' بکشتندشان is the past verb 'they killed' with the enclitic object -شان 'them.' The clause therefore means 'they all killed them before the fire.'
segments.s383-g110: ره بندگی بر نوشتندشان is a causative/inscriptive construction: ره بندگی 'the way/path of servitude' is the thing written, بر is the preposition 'on/upon,' and نوشتندشان is 'they wrote (on) them' — نوشتند with the enclitic -شان as object. The sense is they inscribed or wrote upon the victims the mark/word of servitude (i.e. branded/recorded them as slaves).
segments.s383-g111: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' governing خونشان ('their blood'), where شان is the enclitic pronoun 'their'. بمرد is an archaic verbal form of مردن; here it acts as the main verb meaning 'was extinguished/died' (verb before subject is common in older Persian). آتش زرد ('yellow fire/flame') is the subject phrase and هشت is the old form of هست used here as a copula/auxiliary marking the statement's past/definite aspect; overall the clause means 'from their blood the yellow flame perished/was put out.'
segments.s383-g112: ندانم is the first-person negative of دانستن ('I do not know'). جزا جایشان is a noun phrase meaning 'a recompense/place for them' with جای ('place') plus the enclitic شان ('their'); جزا here means 'recompense' or 'reward.' جز is the exception particle 'except' and بهشت is its complement ('Paradise'). The whole clause therefore reads 'I know of no recompense/place for them except Paradise.'
segments.s383-g97: جز این نیز is a concessive/contrastive phrase meaning 'apart from this / besides this', with نیز 'also' adding emphasis. کاین is a poetic contraction of که این ('that this' / 'that which'), and خود here is emphatic ('that very ...'). پرستنده is an agent noun ('worshipper, devotee') and بود is the past copula ('was'), so the clause states in the past that 'that very devotee was (so)'.
segments.s383-g98: دل is the noun 'heart' (subject or experiencer). از marks separation ('from, away from'), and تاج وز تخت lists the two objects 'crown and throne' in a poetic coordination (وز is a literary connector here). برکنده is a past participle meaning 'detached / torn away' and is predicated with بود (past copula), so برکنده بود = 'had been detached / had torn away (his heart)'.
segments.s383-g99: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. پشت گشتاسپ is a genitive phrase 'Gushtasp's back/support' (پشت here = 'support, backing'). زو is a poetic contraction of ز او ('because of him' / 'from him'), and شد + تهی is a copular construction 'became empty / was emptied'. The clause therefore says that Gushtasp's support was emptied on account of him.
segments.s383-g100: بپیچد is an intransitive verb here meaning 'turn away / turn aside' (3rd-person form used in narrative). ز is the preposition 'from', دیهیم is an adjective/substantive 'rural, rustic' ('the countryside / rural people'), and شاهنشهی is a noun meaning 'kingship / royal/imperial rule'. Together the phrase means that royal authority turned away from the rural populace.
segments.s383-g101: از آنجا 'from there' is an adverbial phrase of origin. به بلخ اندر آمد is a compound expression 'came into Balkh' — به + place 'بلخ' with اندر (archaic 'into/within') plus آمد 'came'. سپاه at the end names the coming entity ('the army'); Persian poetry often places the subject after the verb.
segments.s383-g102: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد as copula). ز introduces cause 'from/because of' (here giving the reason). تاراج و کشتن are nouns/gerunds 'pillage and killing' linked by و. سیاه is the resulting predicative adjective 'black/dark/gloomy' — literally 'the world became dark because of pillage and killing.'
segments.s383-g103: نهادند سر سوی آتشکده is worded with the verb نهادند (past tense, 3rd plural) preceding its object سر; this is a common Classical Persian order where the verb+object combination yields 'they set/placed their heads.' سوی is the preposition 'toward,' and آتشکده is the goal 'fire‑temple.' Together the clause means 'they turned/bowed their heads toward the fire‑temple.'
segments.s383-g104: بران is a deictic/adverbial 'upon that' or 'thereon' linking this noun phrase to the place just mentioned; the phrase lists کاخ و ایوان (palace and iwan/porch). زر qualifies ایوان as 'of gold' (literally 'gold iwan'). آژده is an epithet modifying the building (poetic adjective meaning alarming/awesome). The clause describes what stood at/above the fire‑temple: 'a palace and a golden iwan, awe‑inspiring.'
segments.s383-g25: بخوانم is a 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form used here with a future sense: “I will call/summon.” بدو is not present in this segment; سپاه (subject/object) + پراگنده is an adjectival modifier meaning “scattered.” را is the definite-object marker attaching to the whole noun phrase, so the clause reads “I will summon the scattered host.”
segments.s383-g26: برافشانم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive with a distributive sense here: “I will scatter / lavish (something).” این modifies گنج (this treasure). آگنده is an adjective “full/overflowing,” and را is again the object marker; the whole clause means “I will lavishly distribute this overflowing treasure.”
segments.s383-g27: بدو is the prepositional pronoun “to him.” گفت is the past form “said,” introducing reported speech. کهرم here is a proper name (vocative) and is not one of the supplied lexical senses. که is the complementizer “that,” and فرمان کنم is a verb phrase in the 1st-person present/subjunctive meaning “I should give/issue an order.” The clause therefore presents reported speech: “He said to him, ‘Kahrum, that I should issue an order…’ ”
segments.s383-g28: ز is the poetic preposition “from/of.” فرمان is “command/order,” تو is the 2nd-person pronoun “your,” رامش is an abstract noun here meaning “tranquility/solace,” جان is “soul/life,” and کنم is 1st-person present/subjunctive “I will/may make.” Together: “From your command I will make the solace of my soul.”
segments.s383-g29: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time clause. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. تیغ از میان برکشید is a verbal phrase: برکشید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'drew/raised' and here takes تیغ ('sword') as its object; از میان is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the midst' placed before the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s383-g30: سپاه شب is a noun phrase 'the host/army of night' (noun + modifier). تیره شد uses شد (past of شدن) as a copula/auxiliary meaning 'became' with تیره ('dark') as predicative adjective. ناپدید follows as a second predicate/adverbial adjective meaning 'disappeared/vanished' — a concise poetic sequence of result states.
segments.s383-g31: بیاورد is the past tense 'he/it brought'. کهرم here appears as a proper name or person (placed immediately after the verb in the poem); ز توران is the prepositional phrase 'from Turan' (ز is the poetic equivalent of از). سپاه is the noun 'an army' — object of بیاورد. Poetic word order allows verb + subject + prepositional phrase + object.
segments.s383-g32: جهان گشت is 'the world became/turned' with گشت (گذشته of گشتن) used as 'became'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as'. روی here means 'face' (used metaphorically to mean appearance), followed by زنگی 'Zangi' (an ethnonym meaning a dark-skinned person) and the adjective سیاه 'black/dark' — noun + modifier order is normal in Persian poetry, so 'the world became like the black face of a Zangi.'
segments.s383-g9: از ایدر (prepositional phrase) = "from here." The verb برو (go/went) follows that phrase and precedes the nominative تازیان (the Arabs) for metrical/poetic order: literal sequence 'from here went the Arabs as far as Balkh' (تا به بلخ = 'as far as/to Balkh').
segments.s383-g10: که introduces a causal/subordinate clause ('for/since/that'); از بلخ marks source 'from Balkh.' شد is the past/become verb 'became' here, taking روز ما ('our day/era') as its subject; تار و تلخ are predicative adjectives 'dark/gloomy and bitter,' joined by و. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s383-g11: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'see.' تا introduces the purpose/result clause ('so that / until') and heads the subordinate question phrase کرا یابی 'whom you may find'; یابی is a present/subjunctive/optative form of 'to find' used after تا. از دشمنان ('from/of the enemies') is the source/object of یابی.
segments.s383-g12: This is a noun phrase governed by از ('from/of'): 'from the fire-worshippers and the Ahrimanians.' آتش پرستان is a compound 'fire-worshippers' (آتش + پرستان), و links the coordinated second term آهرمنان (here plural, referring to Ahriman/devils or followers of Ahriman).
segments.s383-g13: Two short imperative commands: سرانشان (their leaders) precedes the verb ببر, which here functions as an imperative 'take/cut off' (i.e. behead). خانهاشان (their khans) precedes the imperative بسوز 'burn!'. Possessive شان is attached to the nouns; poetry favors placing objects before their verbs.
segments.s383-g14: بریشان = بر + ایشان 'upon them' (prepositional phrase marking target). شب آور is an imperative construction where شب ('night') is placed before the verb آور ('bring'); together 'bring night upon them.' به رخشنده روز ('to/against the radiant day') specifies the contrast/goal — turning the bright day into night. Note the verb follows its noun for metrical effect.
segments.s383-g15: از ایوان گشتاسپ is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from Gushtasp's portico' (poetic omission of the ezafe). باید is an impersonal modal 'it is necessary / must,' and که introduces the subordinate clause whose subject is دود ('smoke').
segments.s383-g16: زبانه (literally 'tongues' of flame) is the object/figure associated with دود; برآرد is a third-person singular verb 'bring forth / raise' (here subjunctive/optative after باید که, giving 'must raise'). به چرخ کبود ('to the dark-blue sky') marks the destination. The verb's placement after its object is a poetic inversion.
segments.s383-g41: A string of predicate adjectives joined by و (and). The final -ای on پاینده (pâyande-î) is the 2nd-person copular/predicative ending in this poetic register, so the whole phrase reads as 'you are powerful, wise, and enduring.' The adjectives precede the copula as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s383-g42: An appositive vocative: خداوند (lord) followed by the metaphorical epithet 'خورشید تابنده‌ای'. تابنده‌ای again carries the -ای predicative ending, so the clause means 'O Lord, (you) are a shining sun.' The noun order is noun + epithet + predicative ending.
segments.s383-g43: نگهدار is a vocative/nominative noun meaning 'keeper' or 'protector' addressed to the addressee; دین و تن و هوش are three objects joined by و and modified by the following من (my), so 'protector of my faith, body, and intellect.' The possessive is expressed by placing من after the nouns.
segments.s383-g44: همان ('that, the same') modifies نیروی جان ('the force/strength of the soul' — نیروی is the construct form with جان). وگر is the conditional 'if' and توش is an enclitic/inclusive form meaning 'in it' (تو + ش = 'in it' / 'its inside'); من here is the subject 'I' (so: 'and if I am in it'). The clause is conditional and unfinished, leading into the next line.
segments.s383-g45: که introduces a subordinate/purpose/relative clause ('so that' / 'that'). من بنده is an appositive ('I, a servant'). بر دست ایشان تباه sets up the passive idea ('ruined by their hand') where بر + دست indicates the agent/means ('by their hand'). The verbal predicate is deferred to the next segment.
segments.s383-g46: نگردم is a negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'may I not become' or 'I should not become' (the verb گشتن/گردیدن in subjunctive with negative ن-). توی + پشت = 'in (your) protection/back' where پشت is figurative 'support, protection.' و فریادخواه is a descriptive nominal phrase 'and a petitioner / one who cries for help.' Together they complete the wish started by که: 'may I not be ruined... but be in your protection and a suppliant.'
segments.s383-g47: به بلخ اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/within Balkh' (به + place + اندرون). نامداری is a noun 'renown, fame' and نبود is the past negative copula 'there was not / there was no.' Word order is locative + predicate.
segments.s383-g48: وزان is a conjunctive-prepositional particle 'and from that / and because of that.' گرزداران سواری denotes 'mace-bearers (گرزداران) as mounted troops (سواری).' نبود again is 'there was not.' The phrase states that there were no mounted mace-bearing horsemen.
segments.s383-g89: بدین اندکی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'for/to this little' (ب- = به 'to/for' + this), followed by the subject ما and the question adverb چرا and the past-tense first-person plural آمدیم 'we came'. The clause literally asks 'For this small (thing), why did we come?'.
segments.s383-g90: همی is a poetic adverbial particle (here an emphatic/continuous marker) modifying the verb آمدیم; بی‌گله 'without complaint' is an adverbial phrase; در is the preposition 'into/in'; چرا here is the archaic verbal sense 'to graze' or 'to pasture' (not the interrogative); آمدیم is again 'we came'. Together: 'thus, uncomplaining, we came into (to graze in) the pasture.'
segments.s383-g91: به ترکان is the indirect object 'to the Turks'; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' qualifying what follows; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (third-person), and که here begins the reported content/subordinate clause. Grammatically: 'He said thus to the Turks that ...'.
segments.s383-g92: همین is a demonstrative 'this/this very'; بودمان is a past/copular form with the first-person plural marker (literally 'was to us' or 'we were/it was for us'); رنج 'suffering' is the predicate noun; در کارزار is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the battle/field' specifying the domain of that suffering. The clause states the content of what was said: 'This was our suffering in battle.'
segments.s383-g93: که here introduces a clause ('that'); این نامور شاه is the noun phrase 'this famed king' (demonstrative + adjective + noun); لهراسپ is the proper name; است is the copula 'is'. Word order is subject NP then copula at the end, typical of Persian declarative clauses.
segments.s383-g94: که again links or restates ('and that'); پورش here functions as 'his son' (پور + possessive ـش) in the classical idiom; جهاندار is an epithet 'world-ruler/sovereign'; گشتاسپ is the proper name; است is the copula. The clause says 'and that his son, the sovereign, is Gushtasp.'
segments.s383-g95: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'; با introduces the complementary prepositional phrase 'with/by'; فر یزدان is a compound 'the farr (royal glory) of God/divinity' (فر = divine glory, یزدان = God); بود is the past copula 'was/possessed'. The structure expresses that the sovereign had divine glory.
segments.s383-g96: همه کار او 'all his work/affairs' is the subject phrase; رزم و میدان is a coordinate predicate 'war and the battlefield' (رزم = battle/war, میدان = field/arena); بود is the copula in the past. The sentence means 'All his affairs were combat and the field.'
segments.s383-g1: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb ('now'). رزمِ ارجاسپ را is a definite object phrase (ارجاسپ + را marks it as the object). نو کردن is expressed by placing نو before the verb; کنیم is the 1st-person plural subjunctive/jussive of کردن used as an exhortation 'let us make/renew'.
segments.s383-g2: به + noun phrase here introduces a beneficiary/standard or 'according to' reading ('in/for the nature of the spirit'). باغ is the direct object modified by the compound adjective بی‌خو ('without sleep' = 'sleepless'); کنیم again is 'let us make/bring about'. The clause parallels the first: two coordinated exhortations using کنیم.
segments.s383-g3: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces the subordinate clause with a purposive/result sense ('that/so that'). کهرم is an archaic adjective/noun meaning 'old/ancient' modifying تیغ‌زن 'swordsman' (a seasoned or veteran fighter).
segments.s383-g4: بود functions here as the copula 'was' (past); پیش means 'before/in front of' indicating position relative to سالار 'the commander'. آن انجمن is a determiner + noun phrase 'that assembly' (postposed). The whole clause expresses that the swordsman should be stationed before the assembly's leader.
segments.s383-g5: که here introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('that' / 'for that'). ارجاسپ را uses the topical/object marker را to mark Arjasp as the possessor; بود is the copula 'was'. مهتر پسر is a nominal phrase 'foremost/eldest son' (adjective مهتر before the noun).
segments.s383-g6: به خورشید تابان is a simile introduced by به ('like/to the shining sun'); تابان is a postpositive adjective 'shining'. برآورده is a participial/adjectival form modifying سر 'head' — literally 'head uplifted/raised' — giving the image 'whose head was raised like the shining sun.'
segments.s383-g7: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him' placed before the verb). بگزین is the imperative/jussive 'choose'. ز introduces the source 'from', and لشکر سوار is a genitival/compound expression 'the army's mounted troops' or 'the cavalry'.
segments.s383-g8: ز again 'from'. ترکان is the ethnic identifier 'the Turks' as the group to choose from. شایسته modifies مردی and means 'worthy' or 'fit', and مردی here denotes 'men' (a thousand men). The number 'هزار' is implicit as a quantifier accompanying مردی.
segments.s383-g81: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here (roughly 'just' / 'indeed') placed before the predicate. نوسواریش is the noun نوسوار ('newly mounted/novice rider') with the enclitic -ش meaning 'him' (they thought him to be a n.). پنداشتند is the past plural 'they thought/imagined' — the full clause means 'they imagined him a newly mounted rider.'
segments.s383-g82: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. خود is the emphatic/reflexive pronoun 'themselves'. از + سر is a prepositional construction 'from the head' (سر = head). شاه is 'the king'. برداشتند is past plural 'they took/removed/lifted'. Together this clause reads 'when they themselves lifted (took) the king's head.'
segments.s383-g83: رخی here relates to رخ/رخیدن and functions poetically to indicate a countenance/cheek ('a rosy/ruby face'); the phrase follows the poetic adjective-noun pattern. لعل is 'ruby' used as a simile for color. دیدند is past plural 'they saw'. و links the next noun phrase; کافور موی is an inverted modifier ('hair like camphor' — camphor as a metaphor for whiteness or sheen).
segments.s383-g84: از introduces a material/source relation 'of/from'. آهن سیاه = 'black iron' (material described). آن بهشتیش روی = 'that paradisal face' (آن demonstrative 'that'; بهشتیش is adjectival/possessive form relating to بهشت 'paradise'; روی = 'face'). The phrase says the paradisal face was (made) of black iron — a poetic image.
segments.s383-g85: بماندند is past plural 'they remained/they were left' (stative result). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/utterly'. ازو ('from/of him') marks the person at whom their amazement is directed. در شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in astonishment' — together: 'they were wholly left in astonishment at him.'
segments.s383-g86: که introduces the subordinate clause (here: temporal/causal 'when/that'). این پیر = 'this old man' (subject). شمشیر is the object 'sword'. چون before a verb can mean 'as/when'; برگرفت is past 'he took up/raised' (برگرفتن). So: 'when this old man took up the sword.'
segments.s383-g87: کزین is a fused prepositional demonstrative 'from this' (کز + این). گونه = 'manner, way'. اسفندیار is the proper name being addressed. آمدی is the 2nd-person past 'you came' — an instance of direct address/narrative vocative common in epic style (the hero is addressed as 'you' even in narration).
segments.s383-g88: سپه را places 'the army' as a definite/topic object (را). بر + این + دشت = برین دشت 'upon/to this plain' (بر = on/upon; ین = orthographic variant of این 'this'; دشت = plain). کار آمدی = 'you came for the work/deed' — کار here means the affair/action (battle), and آمدی again is 2nd-person past; the clause means 'you brought the army to this plain to do the deed' or 'you came with the army onto this plain to engage in action.'
segments.s383-g73: Two prepositional/circumstantial phrases giving cause: ز (poetic از) + پیری = 'from/because of old age', then از تابش آفتاب = 'from the radiance/heat of the sun'. تابش آفتاب is a genitive construction (radiance of the sun). These phrases are placed before the verb as the reason for what follows.
segments.s383-g74: غمی گشت is a simple past/resultative: noun (غمی, 'grief') + گشت ('became') = 'became sorrowful'. The conjunction و links the next clause. بخت اندر آمد به خواب is literal word order ('fortune into came to sleep') with اندر آمد (اندر + آمد) meaning 'entered/wasfalling into' and به خواب marking direction/target ('into sleep'), i.e. 'fortune fell asleep' or 'his fortune slumbered'.
segments.s383-g75: جهاندیده is a compound adjective/noun 'world‑seen' (an experienced/worldly‑wise man) serving as topic/subject. از تیر ترکان is an agent/instrument phrase 'by the arrow of the Turks' (از marking source/agent). بخست is an archaic past verb here describing being struck/affected by the arrow (past simple).
segments.s383-g76: This clause reverses the usual modern order: adjective نگونسار ('mournful/woeful') precedes the copula شد ('became') and then the subject مرد یزدان‌پرست ('the God‑worshipping man'). Literal reading: 'mournful became the God‑worshipping man' = 'the pious man became mournful.'
segments.s383-g77: An inverted clause: به خاک اندر آمد (to earth into came) = 'fell to the ground' with به + خاک marking direction and اندر آمد functioning as 'came into/descended'. سر تاجدار is the subject in inverted position = 'the crowned head' (the sovereign's head).
segments.s383-g78: Verb phrase برو انجمن شد ('went/rode to the assembly/happened to go to the assembly') precedes its subject; classical Persian frequently places the verb before subject. فراوان سوار ('many horsemen') is the subject that the verb applies to, so the whole means 'many horsemen went/assembled.'
segments.s383-g79: بکردند is past plural 'they did/made' and governs the following object phrase. چاک آن بر و جوشنش = 'a gash in that robe and his cuirass' (چاک 'gash/tear', آن 'that', بر here contextually the robe/outer garment, و joining جوشنش 'and his mail/armor' with the possessive suffix). Word order places the verb before its object in this poetic line.
segments.s383-g80: Instrumental phrase به شمشیر شد ('by the sword became') uses به + instrument to mark means ('by the sword'), شد as resultative/passive 'was made/became', followed by پاره‌پاره ('in pieces') describing the result and تنش ('his body' with enclitic -ش) as the patient: 'his body was torn to pieces by the sword.'
segments.s383-g65: به ترکان is a prepositional phrase ('to the Turks') placed before the verb as the indirect object; چنین گفت is a fixed narrative formula meaning 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'said'); کهرم is a proper name or vocative inserted before the subordinate clause introduced by که (here naming the person being spoken of) so the whole segment sets up: 'to the Turks he said thus: Kahram...' .
segments.s383-g66: میازید is an older/poetic imperfective/presentive verb form here meaning 'strike/play' (used with چنگ). با او = 'with him / against him' (the preposition + pronoun); یکایک is an adverb 'one by one, each in turn'; به جنگ is a goal/adverbial phrase 'into battle' — the sequence gives the verb plus its accompaniment and manner: 'strike the harp, and with him, one by one, into battle.'
segments.s383-g67: بکوشید here is an imperative (or hortative) plural 'strive!' / 'make effort!'; و is the coordinating 'and'; اندر میانش آورید is another imperative plural 'bring (him) into their midst' — آورید is the plural imperative/command form 'bring', and میانش (میان + -ش) means 'their middle / among them', with اندر 'into/within' marking location.
segments.s383-g68: خروش is the direct object 'the cry/roar'; هژبر ژیان here functions as a compound name or battle-cry (a proper-name/epithet) modifying or apposed to خروش; آورید repeats the imperative plural 'bring/raise' so the clause means 'raise/bring forth the cry “Hežbar Zhiyān.”' In poetic Persian definite markers (ezāfe) are often dropped, so the noun cluster is compact.
segments.s383-g69: برآمد is a past/narrative verb 'arose/issued forth' placed at the start (typical narrative order). چکاچاک is an onomatopoetic/adverbial modifier meaning 'clinking/gleaming' that qualifies what arose; زخم تبر is a compact noun phrase 'the wounds/strikes of the axe' (poetic word order often drops the ezāfe). Together they mean 'the clinking/glittering of axe‑wounds arose.'
segments.s383-g70: This is a verbless nominal clause common in poetry: خروش سواران is 'the tumult/cry of the horsemen' (possessive relation), and پرخاشخر is an attributive adjective 'furious/hostile' (poetic variant of 'پرخاشگر'), so the sense is 'the horsemen's tumult was fierce' with the copula omitted.
segments.s383-g71: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; لهراسپ is the proper name 'Lohrasp' as subject; اندر میانه 'in the middle/midst' gives the location; بماند is a past/narrative form 'remained/was left' (third person), so the clause reads 'When Lohrasp remained in the middle.'
segments.s383-g72: به بیچارگی is an adverbial phrase 'in his misery/helplessness'; نام یزدان is the object 'the name of God' (name + divine name); بخواند is a past/narrative verb 'he called' / 'he cried (out to)' — here it specifically means 'he called on (God)' so the clause says 'in his helplessness he called on the name of God.'
segments.s383-g49: بیامد is a past-tense verb ('he came') placed at the line-beginning (poetic V‑initial order). ز بازار is a prepositional origin phrase ('from the bazaar'). مردی is an indefinite noun ('a man') that is immediately qualified by the following epithet هزار (hyperbolic) — the line uses inversion/ellipses common in verse.
segments.s383-g50: چنانچون is a comparative/concessive particle ('just as / as if'); بود is the past copula ('was' / 'was so') here used to complete the simile. از introduces the source/location and در here functions as 'gate/entrance' (poetic use), so از در کارزار = 'from the gate/entrance of the battlefield.' The whole segment supplies the simile describing the man's appearance.
segments.s383-g51: چو here is a temporal conjunction ('when'). توران سپاه is the subject phrase ('the Turanian army') and اندر آمد is the verb phrase ('entered / came in'); poetic word order places elements for rhythm. به تنگ is a locative phrase ('into a narrow/pressed place' or 'to a narrow pass'), describing where they came.
segments.s383-g52: بپوشید is used as a past action verb ('he donned / put on'); in classical/poetic Persian the verb can precede the subject, so لهراسپ that follows is the actor ('Lohrasp'). خفتان جنگ is the direct object phrase: خفتان (here a noun/nominal form in poetry) is qualified by جنگ ('of war' — a genitive/attributive relation) so the phrase means 'the (war‑)gear / accoutrement of battle' that Lohrasp put on.
segments.s383-g53: ز introduces the origin ('from'); جای پرستش is a genitive noun phrase ('the place of worship' / 'temple'). به introduces the destination ('to') and آوردگاه is a poetic word for 'battlefield' or 'place of muster.' Together this is a full motion phrase 'from the shrine to the field.'
segments.s383-g54: بیامد is again the past tense verb ('he came'). The following noun phrase 'به سر بر کیانی کلاه' describes his appearance on arrival: به سر = 'on (his) head', بر is the relational preposition 'upon/on', کیانی is an epithet ('Kayanian / royal') qualifying کلاه ('cap/helmet'). The phrase thus means 'with a Kayanian helmet on his head' (poetic adjective order places modifiers after the noun).
segments.s383-g55: به plus پیری forms an adverbial phrase ('with an aged / grizzled tone' or 'in the manner of an elder' — a poetic use of پیری). بغرید is past tense 'he roared/bellowed.' چون introduces a simile ('like') and پیل مست ('a drunken elephant') is the vehicle of comparison. The line uses vivid simile: he roared as a drunken elephant would.
segments.s383-g56: یکی is an indefinite pronoun ('one / a certain one'); گرزه is the head noun ('a mace'). گاو پیکر is a compound attributive: گاو ('cow/bull') + پیکر ('bodied/figure') meaning 'bull‑bodied' or 'of the size/shape of a bull' — so 'a bull‑sized mace.' به دست is a locative/instrumental phrase ('in his hand' / 'held in hand'). The whole describes a man holding a huge mace.
segments.s383-g57: به هر حمله‌ای is an adverbial prepositional phrase (به + هر + حمله‌ای) meaning 'at/for every attack'. جادوی is 'jādū‑' (magic/sorcery) with the ezāfe-like ending linking it to the following genitive phrase; زان is an archaic demonstrative/relative ('from that/of those') and سران is the plural 'chiefs/heads'. Together جادوی زان سران = 'the sorcery of those chiefs'.
segments.s383-g58: سپردی is simple past, 2nd person singular 'you handed over/entrusted'. زمین را uses را as the definite object marker after the noun. به + گرز گران is the prepositional phrase 'to the heavy mace(s)' (گرز 'mace/club' + گران 'heavy' post‑positively as adjective). The verb at the start of the segment governs the object زمین را.
segments.s383-g59: همی گفت is a habitual/iterative past construction ('he used to say / he would say'), where همی signals repeated or continuous action. هرکس که این نامدار begins the subordinate relative clause ('whoever/anyone who is not this renowned one') but the negation/predicate follows in the next segment (the clause is split across segments).
segments.s383-g60: نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used here inside the relative clause to mean 'is not' (classical/poetic usage). جز از introduces an exception 'except (from/of)', and گرد اسفندیار here means 'the circle/retinue about Esfandiyar' (گرد used poetically for group/retinue). The clause therefore says 'whoever is not this famed one, except those from Esfandiyar's circle.'
segments.s383-g61: به هر سو is a directional phrase 'toward every direction/whichever way'. که introduces the relative clause 'that/which', and باره here means 'occasion/attack/charge' (an instance of action). برانگیختی is past tense 2nd singular 'you raised/aroused/launched' and is the verb of the clause, so the whole segment reads 'whichever way you set the charge/attack'.
segments.s383-g62: همی here again marks habitual or repeated action ('you would / you always'). خاک با خون is the noun phrase 'earth/soil with blood' (با = 'with'), and برآمیختی is a compound past verb (بر + آمیختی) meaning 'you mingled/you mixed'. The verb completes the earlier adverbial clause to state the recurrent result of the attacks.
segments.s383-g63: هرانکس (fused form of هر کس) + که introduces the relative clause 'whoever/whosoever that'. آواز او یافتی is literally 'his voice reached/you found his voice' — in context it means 'whoever heard his voice/call' (یافتی here functions as past 'reached/was received by you'). This clause sets up the person affected by the action in the next segment.
segments.s383-g64: به تنش = به + تن + ـش (preposition to + 'body' + 3rd‑person enclitic 'his'), i.e. 'to his body'. اندرون = 'inside/within'. زهره here is a poetic term used with 'inside' to indicate the innermost parts; بشکافتی is past 2nd singular of the verb 'to split/cleave' (بشکافتن), so the segment states 'you split open his inner body.'
segments.s383-g33: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (poetic). آمد is the simple past 'came'. بران is a deictic adverb/prepositional element 'to/on that' (i.e. 'to that place/frontier'). مرز is the noun 'border/frontier'. بگشاد is the classical past-form verb 'opened' (بَ + گشاد, found in older/poetic Persian). دست is the noun 'hand' (here part of the idiom 'دست بگشاد' = 'extended his hand' / 'gave help'). The phrase order is typical older Persian: temporal clause (چو آمد بران مرز) followed by the verb phrase (بگشاد دست) where the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s383-g34: کسی را is 'someone' with the object/topic marker را (marks the person as the referent of the following relative clause). که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the clause. بد is the adjective 'bad/ill' modifying the manner of that person's relation. پیش here means 'in the presence of / toward' (i.e. in relation to). آذرپرست is a compound noun 'fire‑worshipper' (a Zoroastrian). The whole segment forms the relative clause 'someone who had been ill (bad) toward the fire‑worshipper'.
segments.s383-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ترکان is the plural subject 'the Turks'. رسیدند is the past plural 'arrived / reached'. نزدیک بلخ is an adverbial phrase 'near Balkh' (نزدیک = 'near', بلخ = the place name Balkh). Word order is temporal marker + subject + verb + place adjunct.
segments.s383-g36: گشاده‌زبان را is the noun 'the outspoken one' with the definite/object marker را. به here introduces manner/instrument ('with' or 'in' a certain manner). گفتار is 'speech' and تلخ is the adjective 'bitter/harsh' (figurative). Together they mean 'they addressed the outspoken man with bitter/harsh words'.
segments.s383-g37: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / on account of' (here introducing the source or cause: 'on hearing of / from ...'). کهرم is a proper name (Kahrām); the provided sense list did not match this use. چو is the temporal 'when'. لهراسپ is the personal name 'Lohrasp'. آگاه is the adjective 'aware' and شد is the past auxiliary 'became' — together 'لهراسپ آگاه شد' = 'Lohrasp became aware' or 'Lohrasp heard/learned'. The phrase order places the causal/source phrase before the temporal clause and the subject-predicate follows.
segments.s383-g38: غمی گشت uses the noun غمی 'a grief' with گشت (past of گشتن) meaning 'became / arose' — 'grief arose'. و is the coordinating 'and'. با رنج همراه شد literally 'became accompanied with suffering' (با رنج = 'with suffering', همراه شد = 'was accompanied'). The construction treats emotional states as subjects that 'become' and then 'are accompanied' by another condition.
segments.s383-g39: به یزدان is the indirect object 'to (the) divine / to God' (به + یزدان). چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'said'); in older Persian گفت is the past of 'to say' (here introducing direct speech). کای is the vocative particle 'O' and کردگار is an epithet 'Creator / Maker' addressed to the divine. The clause order is indirect object + speech verb + direct vocative address.
segments.s383-g40: توی here is the archaic/poetic second‑person 'thou art' (تویی) addressing God—an older inflected form used in poetry. برتر is the comparative/adjective 'higher / superior'. از marks the standard of comparison 'than / from'. گردش is 'turning / revolution' and روزگار is 'time / the course of the world' (here: 'the revolutions/changes of fate/time'). The phrase states the praise 'you are above the turning of time' i.e. above changing fate.
segments.s383-g17: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). بند بر پای اسفندیار is a noun phrase introduced inside that protasis: بند (a fetter/bond) + preposition بر 'on/upon' before پای اسفندیار ('the foot of Esfandiyar'). The whole phrase is the object/goal whose attainment is asserted in the next segment.
segments.s383-g18: بیابی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/optative form of 'to find' used in the protasis ('if you find/obtain'). سرآور functions as a request/commandal vocative/predicate meaning 'bring (victory)/bringer (of success)', and بُرو روزگار is an imperative addressed to روزگار ('O Fate, go!') with برو as an imperative 'go' and روزگار used vocatively as agent of the command.
segments.s383-g19: هم‌آنگه is a temporal adverb ('then/at that moment'). سرش را combines سر 'head' + enclitic possessive ش 'his' and the object marker را to make a definite direct object. ز تن is a prepositional phrase ('from the body') with poetic preposition ز, and بازکن is a 2nd-person singular imperative 'sever/open/undo' (here 'sever') applying to that object.
segments.s383-g20: وزین روی is a descriptive nominal phrase ('with that weighty/dignified countenance' or 'that august face'). گیتی is the object 'the world', and پرآواز کن is an imperative verbal construction where پرآواز ('fame‑giving/renowned') combines with the imperative کن 'do/make' — 'make (him) renowned through the world.' Word order places the descriptive phrase before the world that is to be affected.
segments.s383-g21: همه شهر ایران is the subject phrase 'all the towns/realm of Iran.' به کام تو گشت is the predicate: به کام 'to the desire/will of' + تو 'you' and گشت 'became/turned' (past tense) — together meaning 'fell to/turned to your wish.' The verb follows the oblique complement as is normal in Persian.
segments.s383-g22: This line contains two linked nominal clauses. 'تو تیغی' is a verbless (copula-less) predication: تو 'you' + تیغی (poetic predicate meaning 'sword-like / the sword') — 'you were (like) a sword.' The conjunction و connects the next clause, 'دشمن نیام تو گشت', which inverts normal order for effect: دشمن (enemy) + نیام (scabbard) + تو (you) + گشت (became) — poetically rendered as 'and the enemy became (your) scabbard' (i.e. the enemy was subdued).
segments.s383-g23: من اکنون is the speaker and temporal marker 'I now.' ز خلخ uses poetic preposition ز 'from' with خلخ 'anklet' (likely the jingling sound of an anklet as signal). به اندک زمان is a temporal phrase 'in a short time' (به + اندک زمان) that attaches to the verb in the following segment to indicate immediacy.
segments.s383-g24: بیایم is a 1st-person subjunctive/optative often used to express imminent action ('I will come' / 'may I come'). دمادم is an adverb 'again and again' or 'constantly' modifying the verb. چو 'like/as' + باد 'wind' + دمان 'hem/skirt (of the field or cloak)' form a simile: 'like the wind along the hem' — an image of swift, skimming motion.

sentences.s383-s53: All the living and the place itself burned; because it was richer they attacked it.
sentences.s383-s54: There were eighty men among the Iranians; their speech was full of remembrance of God.
sentences.s383-s55: They all slew them before the fire, and they wrote the path of servitude upon them.
sentences.s383-s56: From their blood the yellow flame was extinguished; I know no recompense for them except Paradise.
sentences.s383-s49: Apart from this, that very devotee had also torn his heart away from crown and throne.
sentences.s383-s50: Now Gushtasp's backing was emptied because of him; royal authority turned away from the rural people.
sentences.s383-s51: From there the army came into Balkh; the world grew dark from pillage and killing.
sentences.s383-s52: They turned their heads toward the fire-temple; upon it stood a palace and a golden iwan, awe‑inspiring.
sentences.s383-s13: I will call together the scattered host; I will lavishly distribute this overflowing treasure.
sentences.s383-s14: He said to him: “Kahrum, that I should give orders — from your command I will make the solace of my soul.”
sentences.s383-s15: When the sun drew its sword from the midst, the host of night darkened and vanished.
sentences.s383-s16: Kahram brought an army from Turan; the world became like the black face of a Zangi.
sentences.s383-s5: From here the Arabs marched as far as Balkh; from Balkh our day became dark and bitter.
sentences.s383-s6: Look and see whom you can find among the enemies — among the fire-worshippers and the followers of Ahriman.
sentences.s383-s7: Behead their leaders, burn their khans; bring night upon them and (turn away) the radiant day.
sentences.s383-s8: From Gushtasp's portico it is necessary that smoke should lift tongues (of flame) to the dark-blue sky.
sentences.s383-s21: You are powerful, wise, and enduring — O Lord, a shining sun.
sentences.s383-s22: Keeper of my religion, body, and wit — that same life-force; and if I am in it...
sentences.s383-s23: so that I, a humble servant, may not be ruined by their hand; (may I be) in your protection and a petitioner for help.
sentences.s383-s24: Within Balkh there was no renown; and from that there were no mace-bearing horsemen.
sentences.s383-s45: Why have we come for so little? Thus, uncomplaining, we came into the pasture.
sentences.s383-s46: He said to the Turks thus: 'Kahram, this was our business — suffering in the battlefield.'
sentences.s383-s47: That this famed king is Lohrasp, and that his son — the world-ruler — is Gushtasp.
sentences.s383-s48: The sovereign possessed the divine farr; all his affairs were war and the battlefield.
sentences.s383-s1: Now let us renew Arjasp's battle; let us, in accordance with the spirit's nature, make the garden sleepless.
sentences.s383-s2: He commanded that a veteran swordsman be (set) before the commander of that assembly.
sentences.s383-s3: For Arjasp had as his foremost son a youth who raised his head like the shining sun.
sentences.s383-s4: He said to him, 'Choose from the army's horsemen a thousand worthy Turk-men.'
sentences.s383-s41: They took him to be a newly mounted rider when they themselves lifted the king's head.
sentences.s383-s42: They saw a ruby-like cheek and camphor-like hair, and that paradisal face was of black iron.
sentences.s383-s43: They were utterly amazed at him when that old man lifted the sword.
sentences.s383-s44: From this manner, Esfandiyar you came; you brought the army to this plain for the deed (i.e., to engage in action).
sentences.s383-s37: Because of old age and the sun's burning he became sorrowful, and fortune fell asleep.
sentences.s383-s38: The worldly-wise one was struck by the Turks' arrow; the pious man became woeful.
sentences.s383-s39: The crowned head fell to the earth; many horsemen went to the assembly.
sentences.s383-s40: They ripped that robe and his coat of mail, and by the sword his body was torn to pieces.
sentences.s383-s33: He said to the Turks thus: “Kahram — strike the harp and fight him, each one, in turn.”
sentences.s383-s34: Strive, and bring him into their midst; lift up the cry of Hežbar Zhiyān.
sentences.s383-s35: The clash and glitter of axe‑blows arose; the tumult of the horsemen was fierce.
sentences.s383-s36: When Lohrasp was left in the midst, in his helplessness he called on the name of God.
sentences.s383-s25: A man came from the bazaar — a man of a thousand — just as if he had come from the gate of the battlefield.
sentences.s383-s26: When the Turanian army came in to the narrow place, Lohrasp put on the accoutrements of war.
sentences.s383-s27: From the place of worship to the battlefield he came, with a Kayanian helmet upon his head.
sentences.s383-s28: He bellowed with a grizzled voice like a drunken elephant; a man carried a bull‑sized mace in his hand.
sentences.s383-s29: At every assault you consigned the land, by the sorcery of those chiefs, to the heavy mace.
sentences.s383-s30: He would say that anyone who is not this renowned man is only from Esfandiyar's circle.
sentences.s383-s31: Whenever you raised an attack toward any direction, you would mingle the earth with blood.
sentences.s383-s32: Whoever heard his cry, you would cleave open the inside of his body.
sentences.s383-s17: When he came to that frontier, he opened his hand to someone who had treated the fire‑worshipper badly.
sentences.s383-s18: When the Turks reached near Balkh, they spoke harshly to the outspoken man.
sentences.s383-s19: When Lohrasp learned of Kahrām, sorrow arose and was joined by suffering.
sentences.s383-s20: To the divine he said thus: 'O Creator, you are above the revolutions of time.'
sentences.s383-s9: If you can fasten a fetter on Esfandiyar's foot, O Fate, go and bring victory.
sentences.s383-s10: Then cut off his head from his body, and with that august countenance make him famed through the world.
sentences.s383-s11: All the towns of Iran turned to your will; you were a sword, and the enemy became your scabbard.
sentences.s383-s12: I will now, at the sound of the anklet, come within a short time — again and again, like the wind along the hem.
